title: Supersymmetry of dissipative Bose-Fermi systems with application to Jaynes-Cummings and Dicke models
abstract: We demonstrate how supersymmetries of Hamiltonians for coupled Bose-Fermi systems can be used to place the Hamiltonians of the Jaynes-Cummings model and Dicke model under the rotating wave approximation in matrix form and provide explicit analytic solutions for their eigenvalues. We then use this supersymmetry to place the Liouvillians of the associated Markovian open systems in matrix form and provide explicit solutions for their eigenvalues. These results are a consequence of the fact that the Hamiltonian of the Jaynes-Cummings model commutes with the linear Casimir invariant of the superalgebra $u(1|1)$ and that the Hamiltonian of the Dicke model commutes both with the linear invariant of $\sum_{i} u_{i}(1|1)$ and with the invariant of an additional $su(2)$ algebra. Our methods apply to various coupled Bose-Fermi systems with $u(1|1)$ and more generally with $u(n|m)$ dynamical superalgebras, and may provide efficient tools for studying more complicated examples.
summary: This paper develops advanced theoretical techniques using supersymmetries to analytically determine the eigenvalues for both the Hamiltonian and the Liouvillian superoperator governing coupled Bose-Fermi systems like the JC and Dicke models. By exploiting underlying algebraic symmetries, the authors reduce large matrix eigenvalue problems into diagonalizing small blocks, providing exact solutions for open quantum dynamics.
main_problem: The primary goal is to find explicit analytic solutions for the eigenvalues of both the Hamiltonian and the Liouvillian superoperator for various coupled Bose-Fermi systems.
main_result: Supersymmetry methods allow the reduction of large eigenvalue problems (for both $H$ and $\mathcal{L}$) into diagonalizing small, manageable block matrices. This yields explicit analytic formulas for the eigenvalues in terms of system parameters.
method: The authors employ techniques from superalgebra theory ($u(n|m)$) to exploit symmetries that commute with conserved quantities (Casimir invariants). The Liouvillian is analyzed by constructing a basis and showing it takes a block-triangular form, allowing diagonalization of smaller blocks.
model_or_system: The study focuses on coupled Bose-Fermi systems, specifically applying the framework to the Jaynes-Cummings (JC) model and the Dicke model. The dynamics are described by Hamiltonians and governed by Lindblad master equations ($\partial_tho = \mathcal{L}ho$).
key_observables: Eigenvalues of the Hamiltonian ($H$) and the Liouvillian superoperator ($\mathcal{L}$); steady-state properties.
important_parameters: Coupling strengths ($g$), cavity frequency ($\omega_c$), qubit/fermion frequency ($\omega_q$), and dissipation rates ($\gamma, \gamma'$).
main_assumptions: The analysis heavily relies on the existence of symmetries (supersymmetries) that commute with the Hamiltonian or Casimir operators, leading to block-diagonalization of the Liouvillian matrix.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spectral properties for JC and Dicke models, showing energy spectra in different parameter regimes and basis representations.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops methods by first solving the Hamiltonian spectrum using superalgebra symmetries. It then extends this methodology to the Liouvillian superoperator, deriving explicit formulas for eigenvalues by analyzing block-diagonal structures arising from bosonic and fermionic dissipation channels.
why_interesting: This work provides powerful mathematical tools—leveraging supersymmetry—to solve complex open quantum system dynamics (Liouvillians) in systems central to cavity QED and quantum optics. The systematic reduction of dimensionality is highly valuable for theoretical modeling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Spectrum of the JC Hamiltonian for g = 0. States are labelled by nB, s.

FIG. 2. (a) Behavior of the eigenvalues of HJC as a function of ζ = ωq/ωc for η = g/ωc = 0. (b) Behavior of the eigenvalues of HJC as a function of ζ for η = 0.2. (c) Behavior of the eigenvalues of HJC as a function of η = g/ωc for ζ = 1 (resonant case).

FIG. 3. Scatterplot of the Jaynes-Cummings model Liouvillian eigenvalues (ωc = 1.0, ωq = 0.5, g = 0.1) for (a) γ = 1.0, γ′ = 0, and (b) γ = 0, γ′ = 1.0. Red points are evaluated from the analytic formula, Eq. (16) and black points are numerical results with NF ock = 20.

FIG. 4. Scatterplot of the Jaynes-Cummings model Liouvil- lian eigenvalues (ωc = 1.0, ωq = 0.5, g = 0.1) for γ = γ′ = 1.0, and NF ock = 20. Red points are evaluated from Eq. (18) and black points are numerical results.
FIG. 5. The uncoupled Dicke spectrum for two qubits. (a) States in a u1(1|1) + u2(1|1) basis. (b) States in a h(2) ⊕su(2)
